title: HashiCorp Certified: Terraform Associate (004)
description: The Terraform Associate certification is for Cloud Engineers specializing in operations, IT, or development who know the basic concepts and skills associated with open source HashiCorp Terraform.

objectives.title: Understand the purpose of Terraform vs other IaC
Use Terraform outside of core workflow

questions.body: Which of the following best describes Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?

choices.text: Manually provisioning infrastructure through a cloud provider's web console
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual processes
Writing scripts in Bash or PowerShell to configure servers after they are provisioned
Using a configuration management tool like Ansible to install software on existing servers

questions.explanation: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable definition files (like Terraform HCL) rather than through manual processes or interactive configuration tools. This approach treats infrastructure configuration the same way developers treat application code — it can be versioned, tested, and reused. Option A describes manual provisioning, which IaC replaces. Option C describes scripting for configuration management, not IaC for provisioning. Option D describes configuration management (a related but distinct practice from IaC).

questions.body: What are the key benefits of using Infrastructure as Code? Select the most complete and accurate answer.

choices.text: Faster provisioning speed only
Version control, repeatability, automation, and documentation of infrastructure
Eliminates the need for cloud providers
Reduces the cost of cloud resources

questions.explanation: The key benefits of IaC are: version control (infrastructure changes are tracked in source control), repeatability (the same configuration produces the same infrastructure), automation (no manual steps), and self-documentation (the code itself describes the infrastructure. Option A is incomplete — speed is one benefit but not the only one. Option C is incorrect — IaC works with cloud providers. Option D is incorrect — IaC doesn't inherently reduce cloud resource costs, though it can help avoid waste through consistency.

questions.body: What is the difference between declarative and imperative approaches to Infrastructure as Code?

choices.text: Declarative IaC requires more code than imperative IaC
Declarative IaC defines the desired end state and the tool figures out how to get there; imperative IaC specifies the exact steps to execute
Imperative IaC is more reliable than declarative IaC
Declarative IaC only works with AWS while imperative IaC works with all providers

questions.explanation: Declarative IaC (like Terraform HCL) lets you define what you want the infrastructure to look like — the desired end state — and the tool determines the sequence of API calls needed to reach that state. Imperative IaC (like Bash scripts or AWS CLI commands) requires you to specify the exact sequence of steps to execute. Terraform uses the declarative approach. Option A is incorrect — declarative code is often more concise. Option C is a false generalization. Option D is completely wrong.

questions.body: Which statement about idempotency in IaC is correct?

choices.text: Running the same Terraform configuration multiple times will always destroy and recreate all resources
Idempotency means applying the same configuration multiple times produces the same result without unintended side effects
Idempotency means each run creates a new copy of the infrastructure
Idempotency is only relevant for configuration management tools, not provisioning tools

questions.explanation: Idempotency means that applying the same operation multiple times yields the same result as applying it once. In Terraform, if your infrastructure already matches the desired configuration, running 'terraform apply' again will result in no changes. This is a core property of declarative IaC tools. Option A is wrong — Terraform will only destroy/recreate resources if the configuration has actually changed from the current state. Option C describes non-idempotent behavior. Option D is incorrect — idempotency is relevant for all IaC tools.

questions.body: A team currently uses manual processes to provision cloud resources, which has led to configuration drift and inconsistent environments. Which IaC benefit most directly addresses this problem?

choices.text: Faster execution compared to manual provisioning
Reduced cloud provider billing
Consistency and repeatability — the same code always produces the same infrastructure
Automatic security scanning of infrastructure

questions.explanation: Configuration drift occurs when infrastructure deviates from its intended state, often due to manual changes made outside the IaC tool. The IaC benefit that most directly addresses this is consistency and repeatability — because all infrastructure is defined in code, every environment is provisioned from the same source, eliminating the variance caused by manual processes. Option A (speed) and B (billing) don't address the drift/inconsistency problem. Option D is not a built-in IaC benefit — security scanning is a separate concern.

questions.body: What is the primary advantage of Terraform over cloud-provider-specific IaC tools like AWS CloudFormation?

choices.text: Terraform is faster at provisioning resources
Terraform is multi-cloud and provider-agnostic, allowing you to manage resources across different providers with a single workflow
Terraform is officially supported by all cloud providers
Terraform templates are written in YAML which is easier to read

questions.explanation: Terraform's primary advantage over provider-specific tools like CloudFormation (AWS-only), ARM templates (Azure-only), or Deployment Manager (GCP-only) is that it is provider-agnostic. You can manage resources across AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and hundreds of other providers using the same Terraform workflow and language (HCL). Option A is not a defined or guaranteed advantage. Option C is incorrect — Terraform is developed by HashiCorp, not cloud providers. Option D is wrong — Terraform uses HCL, not YAML.

questions.body: How does Terraform differ from configuration management tools like Ansible, Chef, or Puppet?

choices.text: Terraform cannot manage cloud resources; it only manages on-premises servers
Terraform focuses on provisioning infrastructure (creating/destroying resources), while configuration management tools focus on configuring software on existing servers
Ansible and Puppet can create cloud resources but Terraform cannot
Configuration management tools use a push model and Terraform uses a pull model

questions.explanation: Terraform is primarily an infrastructure provisioning tool — it excels at creating, modifying, and destroying infrastructure resources like VMs, networks, databases, and DNS records. Configuration management tools like Ansible, Chef, and Puppet focus on configuring software within already-provisioned servers (installing packages, managing files, running services). These tools are complementary — Terraform provisions the server, then a CM tool configures it. Option A is wrong — Terraform manages cloud resources extensively. Option C is incorrect. Option D is inaccurate about their models.

questions.body: A company wants to use Terraform to manage both their AWS infrastructure and their Kubernetes cluster configurations. What feature of Terraform enables this?

choices.text: Terraform modules
The Terraform provider ecosystem, which includes providers for AWS, Kubernetes, and hundreds of other platforms
Terraform workspaces
The terraform import command

questions.explanation: Terraform's provider ecosystem is what enables managing multiple different platforms with the same tool. A Terraform provider is a plugin that translates Terraform resource definitions into API calls for a specific platform. There are providers for AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, GitHub, Datadog, and thousands more — all available on the Terraform Registry. Option A (modules) are for code reuse, not multi-platform support. Option C (workspaces) manage multiple instances of the same configuration. Option D (terraform import) brings existing resources under Terraform management.

questions.body: What is Terraform Cloud and what benefit does it provide over running Terraform locally?

choices.text: Terraform Cloud is a cloud provider, like AWS or Azure, for deploying infrastructure
Terraform Cloud provides remote state storage, a remote execution environment, team collaboration features, and a policy framework for Terraform runs
Terraform Cloud is only available to enterprise customers and provides no benefits to small teams
Terraform Cloud automatically writes Terraform configurations based on your existing infrastructure

questions.explanation: Terraform Cloud is HashiCorp's SaaS platform for teams using Terraform. It provides remote state management (no need for manual S3/Azure Blob backend setup), a consistent remote execution environment, team access controls, VCS integration for GitOps workflows, policy enforcement with Sentinel, and a private module registry. These features address the challenges teams face when running Terraform locally — like conflicting state files and inconsistent execution environments. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect descriptions of Terraform Cloud.

questions.body: Which of the following scenarios is MOST suitable for using Terraform?

choices.text: Installing and configuring Nginx on an existing EC2 instance
Deploying application code to a running server
Provisioning a VPC, subnets, security groups, and EC2 instances across multiple AWS regions
Managing cron jobs on Linux servers

questions.explanation: Terraform is purpose-built for provisioning and managing cloud infrastructure resources like VPCs, subnets, security groups, compute instances, databases, and load balancers. Provisioning infrastructure across multiple regions is an ideal Terraform use case. Option A (configuring Nginx on an existing server) is better handled by Ansible or a similar CM tool. Option B (deploying application code) is handled by CI/CD tools like Jenkins or GitHub Actions. Option D (managing cron jobs) is a configuration management task, not infrastructure provisioning.

questions.body: What does the HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) offer compared to JSON for writing Terraform configurations?

choices.text: HCL supports more Terraform features than JSON
HCL is human-friendly with support for comments, and more readable syntax while still being machine-parseable; JSON is also valid but more verbose
JSON configurations run faster than HCL configurations
Only HCL can be used with Terraform providers; JSON cannot

questions.explanation: Terraform supports both HCL (.tf files) and JSON (.tf.json files) as configuration formats. HCL was designed to be human-friendly — it supports comments, has a cleaner syntax for nested structures, and is more readable than JSON. JSON is more verbose and doesn't support comments but is useful when configurations are generated programmatically. Both formats have the same capabilities. Option A is incorrect — both support the same features. Option C is false. Option D is wrong — JSON configurations work with all providers.

questions.body: What is a Terraform provider and what is its primary purpose?

choices.text: A provider is a Terraform configuration file that defines resource variables
A provider is a plugin that enables Terraform to interact with a specific API or platform, translating resource definitions into API calls
A provider is a cloud account configuration that stores credentials
A provider is a Terraform module published to the public registry

questions.explanation: A Terraform provider is a plugin that acts as a translation layer between Terraform resource definitions and a specific API or platform. When you define an 'aws_instance' resource, the AWS provider handles the API calls to create, read, update, or delete that EC2 instance. Providers are responsible for understanding API interactions and exposing resources and data sources. Option A confuses providers with variables. Option C confuses providers with provider configurations (the block where you configure credentials). Option D confuses providers with modules.

choices.text: Terraform will create all Azure resources defined in the configuration
Terraform will download and install the AzureRM provider plugin version ~> 3.0 from the Terraform Registry
Terraform will validate the Azure credentials
Terraform will create a plan showing what resources will be created

questions.explanation: 'terraform init' is the initialization command that prepares a working directory. Its primary job is to download and install the provider plugins specified in the 'required_providers' block. In this case, it would download the hashicorp/azurerm provider, constrained to version ~> 3.0 (any 3.x version). Option A is wrong — 'terraform apply' creates resources. Option C is partially true in that it validates the configuration but does not check actual Azure credentials. Option D describes 'terraform plan'.

questions.body: What is the purpose of the `.terraform.lock.hcl` file?

choices.text: It stores the Terraform state, recording all managed resources
It locks the Terraform state file to prevent concurrent modifications
It records the exact provider versions and checksums selected during 'terraform init' to ensure consistent provider versions across all team members
It prevents unauthorized users from running 'terraform apply'

questions.explanation: The .terraform.lock.hcl file is a dependency lock file automatically created by 'terraform init'. It records the exact provider versions selected (even within a version constraint range) and their cryptographic checksums. This ensures that all team members and CI/CD pipelines use the exact same provider versions, preventing 'works on my machine' problems. This file should be committed to version control. Option A describes terraform.tfstate. Option B describes state locking (a different mechanism). Option D is not a Terraform feature.

choices.text: Resource blocks are for Azure only; data blocks work with all providers
Resource blocks define infrastructure to be created/managed by Terraform; data blocks query existing infrastructure or external data sources without managing their lifecycle
Data blocks are deprecated and should be replaced with resource blocks
Resource blocks and data blocks are identical; the names are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Resource blocks declare infrastructure objects that Terraform will create and manage throughout their lifecycle (create, update, delete). Data source blocks allow Terraform to read information from existing infrastructure or external sources without taking ownership of those resources — Terraform will never destroy a data source. In the example, the data block looks up an existing Azure resource group and the resource block creates a new Linux VM in it. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect — resources and data sources are distinct concepts used across all providers.

questions.body: Which version constraint in Terraform allows any minor or patch version of 3.x but NOT version 4.0?
questions.explanation: The '~>' operator is the 'pessimistic constraint operator' in Terraform. '~> 3.0' allows versions >= 3.0 and < 4.0, accepting any 3.x version. More precisely, it allows the rightmost version component to increase but keeps the version within the same major version. Option A (>= 3.0) would also allow 4.0, 5.0, etc. Option C (= 3.0) only allows exactly version 3.0. Option D (!= 4.0) would also allow 2.x, 1.x, and anything other than 4.0.

questions.body:
Consider the following Terraform variable declaration. What will happen if a user runs `terraform apply` without providing a value for this variable?

```hcl
variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
}
```

choices.text: Terraform will use 't2.micro' as the default value
Terraform will fail with an error saying the variable has no default value
Terraform will prompt the user interactively to provide a value
Terraform will use an empty string as the value

questions.explanation: When a variable has no default value and no value is provided via CLI flags (-var), .tfvars files, or environment variables (TF_VAR_), Terraform will interactively prompt the user to enter a value when running terraform plan or terraform apply. If running in a non-interactive environment (like CI/CD), this will cause the command to hang or fail. Option A is wrong — there is no built-in default. Option B is not what happens (no error, just a prompt). Option D is incorrect.

questions.body: What is the correct syntax to reference the `id` attribute of an `azurerm_virtual_network` resource named `main` in Terraform?

choices.text: ${azurerm_virtual_network.id.main}
var.azurerm_virtual_network_main_id
azurerm_virtual_network.main.id
resource.azurerm_virtual_network.main.id

questions.explanation: The correct syntax to reference a resource attribute in Terraform is: <resource_type>.<resource_name>.<attribute>. So to reference the 'id' of an 'azurerm_virtual_network' resource named 'main', you use 'azurerm_virtual_network.main.id'. This is called a resource reference or a resource attribute expression. Option A uses incorrect bracket syntax and wrong order. Option B is variable syntax. Option D incorrectly includes 'resource.' prefix — that prefix is not used in attribute references.

questions.body:
What type of dependency does the following configuration create, and how does Terraform handle it?

```hcl
resource "azurerm_subnet" "public" {
  name                 = "public-subnet"
  resource_group_name  = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.main.name
  address_prefixes     = ["10.0.1.0/24"]
}
```

choices.text: An explicit dependency using the depends_on meta-argument; Terraform requires depends_on for all resource relationships

An implicit dependency through the references to azurerm_resource_group.main and azurerm_virtual_network.main; Terraform automatically detects these and ensures those resources are created before the subnet
A circular dependency that will cause terraform apply to fail
No dependency is created; Terraform creates resources in alphabetical order

questions.explanation: When you reference one resource's attribute in another resource's argument (e.g., virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.main.name), Terraform automatically detects an implicit dependency. The dependency graph ensures azurerm_resource_group.main and azurerm_virtual_network.main are created first, and then azurerm_subnet.public is created using their actual values. This is the preferred way to express dependencies in Terraform. Option A describes 'depends_on', which is for explicit dependencies when the relationship isn't expressed through references. Option C is wrong — these are valid directional dependencies. Option D is wrong — Terraform uses the dependency graph, not alphabetical order.

questions.body: What is a Terraform local value and when should you use it?

choices.text: Local values store sensitive data like passwords and are encrypted in state
Local values are input variables that users provide when running terraform apply
Local values allow you to assign a name to an expression for reuse within a module, reducing repetition and improving readability
Local values are only available in the root module and cannot be used in child modules

questions.explanation: Local values (defined in a 'locals' block) let you compute intermediate values or assign a name to a complex expression, then reference that name multiple times within the same module. For example: locals { full_name = '${var.prefix}-${var.name}' }. This avoids repeating the same expression and makes the configuration easier to maintain. Option A is incorrect — locals are not encrypted and should not store sensitive data. Option B describes input variables. Option D is wrong — locals can be defined in any module.

choices.text: Create one virtual machine and name it 'web-3'
Create 3 identical Linux virtual machines named web-0, web-1, and web-2
Create 3 virtual machines but only the first one will have a Name tag
Cause a Terraform error because count.index is not a valid expression

questions.explanation: The 'count' meta-argument creates multiple instances of a resource. With count = 3, Terraform creates 3 azurerm_linux_virtual_machine resources. Within the resource block, 'count.index' refers to the current instance's index (0, 1, or 2). The name expression '"web-${count.index}"' will produce 'web-0', 'web-1', and 'web-2' respectively. The resources are referenced as azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web[0], azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web[1], azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web[2]. Option A is wrong about the count. Option C is wrong — all instances get Name tags. Option D is wrong — count.index is valid.

questions.body: What is the difference between `count` and `for_each` in Terraform, and when should you prefer `for_each`?

choices.text: count and for_each are identical; use either one

for_each accepts a map or set and creates resources keyed by the map keys; prefer for_each when resources have distinct identities because removing a middle item won't force recreation of subsequent items
count is preferred because for_each doesn't work with modules
for_each can only be used when the number of resources is known at plan time, while count works with computed values

questions.explanation: Both count and for_each create multiple instances of a resource, but they differ importantly. 'count' creates a list indexed by integers (0, 1, 2...). If you remove the middle item, Terraform renumbers subsequent items, which can cause unintended destroy/recreate cycles. 'for_each' creates a map indexed by string keys. Removing one entry only affects that specific resource, leaving others unchanged. Prefer for_each when each instance has a stable identity. Option A is wrong — they have important behavioral differences. Option C is wrong — for_each works with modules. Option D has the constraint backwards.

questions.body: What does the `terraform output` command do?

choices.text: Displays the full Terraform state file content
Shows the values of output variables defined in the configuration after they have been applied
Generates the execution plan without applying changes
Exports provider credentials to environment variables

questions.explanation: The 'terraform output' command reads and displays the values of output variables defined in the root module's output blocks. These values are computed after a successful 'terraform apply' and stored in the state file. This command is useful for retrieving values like IP addresses, resource IDs, or DNS names from applied infrastructure. You can also use '-json' flag for machine-readable output. Option A describes the state file itself. Option C describes 'terraform plan'. Option D is not a Terraform feature.

questions.body: Which of the following is a valid Terraform output value definition for an Azure virtual network?

choices.text: output "vnet_id" { value = azurerm_virtual_network.main.id }
export "vnet_id" = azurerm_virtual_network.main.id
return azurerm_virtual_network.main.id as vnet_id
output = { vnet_id = azurerm_virtual_network.main.id }

questions.explanation: The correct Terraform syntax for an output value is: output "<name>" { value = <expression> }. The output block takes a label (the name) and requires the 'value' argument. Optional arguments include 'description', 'sensitive', and 'depends_on'. Option B, C, and D all use invalid syntax that is not valid HCL or Terraform.

questions.body: What happens to `output` values marked as `sensitive = true` in Terraform?

choices.text: The output is encrypted in the state file
The output value is redacted in CLI output (shown as <sensitive>), but the value is still stored in plain text in the state file
Sensitive outputs cannot be passed to child modules
Terraform refuses to store sensitive outputs in state and they must be retrieved from the provider each time

questions.explanation: When an output is marked 'sensitive = true', Terraform will suppress its value in CLI output — it shows '<sensitive>' instead of the actual value. This prevents accidental exposure of secrets in terminal output or CI/CD logs. However, the value is still stored in plain text in the terraform.tfstate file and is accessible via 'terraform output -json'. It's critical to protect the state file when sensitive values are stored. Option A is incorrect — state is not encrypted. Option C is wrong — sensitive outputs can be passed to modules. Option D is incorrect.

questions.body: What does `terraform taint` (or its modern equivalent `terraform apply -replace`) do?

choices.text: Marks a resource as permanently invalid so it will be destroyed and never recreated
Forces a resource to be destroyed and recreated on the next 'terraform apply', even if the configuration hasn't changed
Removes a resource from the Terraform state without destroying the actual infrastructure
Validates that a resource is in the correct state without making changes

questions.explanation: 'terraform taint' (deprecated in favor of 'terraform apply -replace=<address>') marks a resource as tainted, meaning Terraform will plan to destroy and recreate it on the next apply, regardless of whether the configuration has changed. This is useful when a resource is in a known-bad state (e.g., an EC2 instance with a corrupted filesystem) and needs to be replaced. Option A is wrong — the resource will be recreated. Option C describes 'terraform state rm'. Option D describes 'terraform plan' or validation.

questions.body: A database instance exists in your cloud environment and was not originally created by Terraform. How can you bring it under Terraform management without destroying and recreating it?

choices.text: Write a resource block for it in your configuration and run terraform apply — Terraform will automatically detect the existing resource
Use 'terraform import' to bring the existing resource into Terraform state, then write the corresponding resource block
Use 'terraform refresh' to import all existing cloud resources automatically
It is not possible to manage existing resources with Terraform

questions.explanation: 'terraform import' allows you to bring existing infrastructure under Terraform management. The process is: 1) Write the resource block in your configuration describing the resource. 2) Run 'terraform import <resource_address> <provider_resource_id>' to add the resource to Terraform's state file. After import, run 'terraform plan' to verify the configuration matches actual state. Option A is wrong — Terraform would try to create a new duplicate resource. Option C is wrong — 'terraform refresh' updates state for already-managed resources, it does not import new resources. Option D is incorrect.

questions.body: What does `terraform validate` check?

choices.text: Whether your cloud provider credentials are valid
Whether the configuration is syntactically valid and internally consistent, without accessing any remote services
Whether the planned infrastructure changes will succeed before applying them
Whether the state file is consistent with actual infrastructure

questions.explanation: 'terraform validate' checks that the configuration files are syntactically valid HCL and that the configuration is internally consistent — for example, that referenced variables are declared, that resource types and argument names are correct, and that required arguments are provided. Importantly, it does NOT make any API calls to cloud providers. It requires 'terraform init' to have been run first. Option A requires actual provider API calls. Option C describes 'terraform plan'. Option D describes 'terraform refresh'.

questions.body: When should you use `terraform state mv`?

choices.text: To move a resource from one cloud region to another
To rename a resource in Terraform state without destroying and recreating it, such as when refactoring module structure
To migrate state from local backend to a remote backend
To move a resource to a different Terraform workspace

questions.explanation: 'terraform state mv' moves an item within the Terraform state, changing the resource address without affecting the actual infrastructure. This is useful when: renaming a resource in your configuration, moving a resource into or out of a module, or refactoring module structure. Without this command, a resource rename would cause Terraform to plan a destroy/create cycle. Option A is not possible with this command — moving to another region requires destroy and recreate. Option C is done by changing the backend configuration. Option D is not how workspaces work.

questions.body: What does the `terraform fmt` command do?

choices.text: Formats Terraform output values for display in the terminal
Rewrites Terraform configuration files to a canonical format and style, fixing indentation and alignment
Formats the Terraform plan as a JSON document
Converts Terraform configurations between HCL and JSON formats

questions.explanation: 'terraform fmt' is a formatting command that automatically rewrites Terraform configuration files (.tf and .tfvars) to the canonical format defined by the Terraform style guide — fixing indentation, aligning equals signs in attribute assignments, and consistent spacing. Running 'terraform fmt -check' in CI/CD pipelines verifies that code is properly formatted. It modifies files in-place. Option A, C, and D all describe things that terraform fmt does not do.

questions.body: You need to immediately destroy a specific resource without modifying your configuration file. Which command accomplishes this?

choices.text: terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web
terraform delete aws_instance.web
terraform remove aws_instance.web
terraform apply -destroy

questions.explanation: 'terraform destroy -target=<resource_address>' destroys only the specified resource (and resources that depend on it) without modifying the configuration file. The -target flag can be used with both plan, apply, and destroy to operate on a subset of resources. Option B and C are not valid Terraform commands. Option D ('terraform apply -destroy') is equivalent to 'terraform destroy' but would plan to destroy ALL resources in the configuration, not just the specific one.

questions.body: What is the purpose of `terraform refresh` (now largely superseded by the `-refresh-only` plan option)?

choices.text: Downloads the latest version of all providers
Updates the Terraform state file to reflect the actual current state of real-world infrastructure, without making any infrastructure changes
Refreshes the Terraform provider cache
Re-runs the last terraform apply to bring infrastructure back to the desired state

questions.explanation: 'terraform refresh' queries the real-world infrastructure and updates the state file to reflect the current actual state, without making any changes to the infrastructure itself. This is useful when infrastructure has been modified outside of Terraform (configuration drift). The modern equivalent is 'terraform apply -refresh-only' or 'terraform plan -refresh-only', which are safer because they show you what changed before updating state. Option A is 'terraform init -upgrade'. Options C and D are incorrect.

questions.body: Which lifecycle meta-argument prevents Terraform from destroying a resource even if the configuration is removed or requires replacement?

choices.text: create_before_destroy = true
ignore_changes = [all]
prevent_destroy = true
force_new = false

questions.explanation: The 'prevent_destroy = true' lifecycle setting will cause Terraform to return an error if any plan would destroy that resource. This is a safety guard for critical resources like production databases. If Terraform determines it needs to destroy the resource (either due to a configuration change requiring replacement, or because the resource block was removed), it will fail the plan with an error rather than proceeding. Option A (create_before_destroy) changes the ordering of replacement, not prevention. Option B (ignore_changes) ignores drift, not destruction. Option D is not a valid Terraform argument.

questions.body: What does `create_before_destroy = true` do in a Terraform lifecycle block?

choices.text: Forces Terraform to always create the resource even if it already exists
When a resource needs to be replaced, Terraform creates the new resource first and then destroys the old one, reducing downtime
Prevents the resource from being destroyed if any other resources depend on it
Creates a backup of the resource before destroying it

questions.explanation: By default, when Terraform needs to replace a resource (destroy and recreate due to a change in an immutable attribute), it destroys the old resource first, then creates the new one — causing a brief outage. With 'create_before_destroy = true', Terraform reverses this order: it creates the new resource first, then destroys the old one. This is particularly useful for resources that have downstream dependencies (like a new EC2 instance replacing an old one behind a load balancer). Option A is incorrect. Option C describes 'prevent_destroy'. Option D is not a Terraform feature.

questions.body: What does `terraform state list` do?

choices.text: Shows a list of all available Terraform backends
Lists all resources tracked in the current Terraform state file
Shows available Terraform versions
Lists all files in the current Terraform working directory

questions.explanation: 'terraform state list' shows all resources and data sources currently tracked in the Terraform state file, displaying their full resource addresses (e.g., 'aws_instance.web', 'module.vpc.aws_subnet.public[0]'). This is useful for understanding what resources are under Terraform management and for getting the exact resource address needed for commands like 'terraform state show', 'terraform state mv', or 'terraform apply -target'. Options A, C, and D describe things that terraform state list does not do.

questions.body: What is a Terraform module and what primary benefit does it provide?

choices.text: A module is a single Terraform resource block that can be reused
A module is a container of multiple Terraform resources that are used together, enabling code reuse, abstraction, and composability
A module is a paid feature of Terraform Enterprise only
A module is a Python script that extends Terraform's functionality

questions.explanation: A Terraform module is a collection of .tf files in a directory that are managed together. Modules encapsulate groups of resources, allowing you to reuse infrastructure patterns across projects, create abstractions that hide complexity, and enforce organizational standards. The 'root module' is your main working directory; 'child modules' are called using module blocks. Modules can be local (a directory on disk) or remote (Terraform Registry, Git repos). Options A, C, and D are all incorrect descriptions.

questions.body: How do you pass values into a child module and retrieve values from it?

choices.text: Child modules automatically inherit all variables from the parent module

Values are passed into a module using input variables (declared with 'variable' blocks in the module) and set via arguments in the 'module' block. Values are returned using 'output' blocks in the module and referenced as module.<name>.<output_name>
Modules communicate using environment variables that Terraform sets automatically
You must use data sources to retrieve values from child modules

questions.explanation: Module communication in Terraform follows a clear pattern: 1) The child module declares 'variable' blocks for each input it accepts. 2) The parent module calls the child using a 'module' block and sets those variables as arguments. 3) The child module declares 'output' blocks for values it wants to expose. 4) The parent references those outputs as 'module.<module_name>.<output_name>'. This explicit interface makes module dependencies clear and testable. Options A, C, and D all describe incorrect mechanisms.

questions.body: What is the Terraform Registry and what can you find there?

choices.text: The Terraform Registry (registry.terraform.io) is where providers and publicly shared modules are published and discovered
The Terraform Registry is a local cache of downloaded providers
The Terraform Registry is an internal HashiCorp tool only available with an enterprise license
The Terraform Registry stores Terraform state files for remote backends

questions.explanation: The Terraform Registry (registry.terraform.io) is the public hub for discovering and distributing Terraform providers and modules. Providers published on the registry can be sourced using the 'hashicorp/aws' notation. Community and verified modules can be used directly in configurations with a registry source address. Terraform Cloud also supports a private registry for internal modules. Options B, C, and D are all incorrect descriptions.

questions.body:
Consider the following module call. What does the `source` argument do and when is `terraform init` required after adding this?

```hcl
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.0.0"

  name = "my-vpc"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
}
```

choices.text: The source argument is optional; Terraform downloads modules automatically without terraform init
The source argument specifies where to download the module from. terraform init must be re-run whenever a new module source is added or the version is changed to download the module locally
The source argument points to a local directory only; registry sources are not supported in this syntax
The version argument is ignored for registry modules; the latest version is always used

questions.explanation: The 'source' argument tells Terraform where to find the module — this can be the Terraform Registry (as in this example), a GitHub URL, a local path, or other sources. The 'version' argument constrains which module version to use (registry modules only). After adding or changing a module source, you MUST run 'terraform init' to download the module into the .terraform directory. Option A is wrong — init is required. Option C is wrong — registry sources are fully supported. Option D is wrong — the version constraint is respected.

questions.body: What is the difference between a public module from the Terraform Registry and a private module in Terraform Cloud's private registry?

choices.text: There is no difference; all modules work the same way

Public registry modules are open-source and visible to everyone. Private registry modules are only accessible to members of your Terraform Cloud organization and can contain proprietary infrastructure patterns
Private modules cannot have input variables or outputs
Private registry modules must be written in JSON, not HCL

questions.explanation: The public Terraform Registry (registry.terraform.io) hosts open-source modules that anyone can discover and use. Terraform Cloud's private registry lets organizations publish and share modules internally — these modules are only accessible to members of the organization and require authentication. The private registry supports the same versioning, documentation, and input/output specifications as the public registry. Options C and D are completely incorrect — all modules support variables and HCL.

questions.body: After running `terraform init` with a module from the Terraform Registry, where are the downloaded module files stored?

choices.text: In the same directory as the root module configuration files
In the .terraform/modules directory within your working directory
In a global cache at ~/.terraform/modules
In the /tmp directory and deleted after each plan/apply

questions.explanation: When 'terraform init' downloads modules, it stores them in the '.terraform/modules/' directory within the current working directory. This directory should generally NOT be committed to version control (add it to .gitignore). The provider downloads go into '.terraform/providers/'. The .terraform directory as a whole is the working cache for the current configuration. Option A would create clutter and conflicts. Option C describes a potential plugin cache location but not module storage. Option D is incorrect.

questions.body: What happens when you run `terraform get` in a Terraform working directory?

choices.text: Downloads and installs provider plugins
Downloads and updates modules referenced in the configuration without reinitializing providers
Retrieves the current state from the remote backend
Gets the list of available Terraform versions

questions.explanation: 'terraform get' downloads and updates modules referenced in the configuration. Unlike 'terraform init', it only handles module downloads and does not reinitialize provider plugins or backend configuration. Running 'terraform init' also downloads modules, so 'terraform get' is generally used when you want to update modules without reinitializing everything. Option A describes part of 'terraform init'. Option C is not what 'terraform get' does. Option D is not a Terraform command.

questions.body: A module is defined at `./modules/networking`. How would you call this local module from your root configuration?

choices.text: module "networking" { source = "networking" }
module "networking" { source = "./modules/networking" }
include "./modules/networking"
import module from "./modules/networking"

questions.explanation: To reference a local module, the 'source' argument must be a relative path starting with './' or '../'. Using 'source = "./modules/networking"' tells Terraform to look for the module in the 'modules/networking' subdirectory relative to the current configuration. Option A without './' is not a valid relative path for a local module (it would be interpreted as a registry module). Options C and D are not valid Terraform syntax.

questions.body: What is the correct order of the core Terraform workflow?

choices.text: Write → Plan → Apply
Init → Plan → Apply → Destroy
Write → Init → Plan → Apply
Plan → Write → Init → Apply

questions.explanation: The core Terraform workflow is: 1) Write — author infrastructure configuration in .tf files. 2) Init — run 'terraform init' to initialize the working directory, download providers and modules. 3) Plan — run 'terraform plan' to see what changes Terraform will make. 4) Apply — run 'terraform apply' to execute the plan and make the actual changes. This workflow is a repeating loop — after applying, you go back to write to make more changes. Options A (missing Init), B (missing Write), and D (wrong order) are all incomplete or incorrect.

questions.body: When you run `terraform plan`, what does Terraform do to generate the execution plan?

choices.text: Only reads the configuration files without checking current state
Reads the configuration, reads the current state (from state file and optionally refreshes from real infrastructure), then calculates the diff to determine what changes need to be made
Creates all resources immediately and rolls back if any fail
Compiles the HCL into a binary executable

questions.explanation: During 'terraform plan', Terraform: 1) Reads all .tf configuration files in the working directory. 2) Reads the current state file (and by default refreshes it by querying the actual cloud provider). 3) Compares the desired state (configuration) with the current state to produce a diff. 4) Presents the planned actions (create, update, destroy) without actually making any changes. The plan is deterministic — running plan again with the same configuration and state produces the same result. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect.

questions.body: How can you save a Terraform execution plan to a file and then apply exactly that plan?

choices.text: terraform plan > plan.txt && terraform apply plan.txt
terraform plan -out=tfplan && terraform apply tfplan
terraform save plan.json && terraform apply plan.json
terraform plan --save && terraform apply --saved

questions.explanation: 'terraform plan -out=<filename>' saves the execution plan to a binary file. When you then run 'terraform apply <filename>', Terraform applies exactly that saved plan without re-running the planning phase and without asking for confirmation. This is important for CI/CD workflows and compliance — you can separate the approval step (reviewing the plan) from the execution step. Option A saves human-readable text but not the binary plan. Options C and D use invalid syntax.

questions.body: What does `terraform destroy` do and what is the safer alternative for targeted removal?

choices.text: 'terraform destroy' deletes the state file, and there is no alternative

'terraform destroy' destroys ALL resources in the Terraform configuration. For targeted removal, use 'terraform destroy -target=<resource>' or remove the specific resource block and run 'terraform apply'
'terraform destroy' only removes resources from state without affecting real infrastructure
'terraform destroy' is identical to 'terraform apply'

questions.explanation: 'terraform destroy' plans and executes the destruction of all resources managed by the current Terraform configuration. It is equivalent to running 'terraform apply -destroy'. This is typically used to clean up development or test environments. For removing specific resources, you can use 'terraform destroy -target=<resource_address>' or simply delete the resource block from the configuration and run 'terraform apply' — Terraform will then plan the removal of only that resource. Option A is wrong — state is updated, not deleted. Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong.

questions.body: What is the Terraform state file and why is it important?

choices.text: The state file is a log of all Terraform commands ever run
The state file records the mapping between your Terraform resources and the real-world infrastructure objects they represent, enabling Terraform to plan changes and track resource lifecycle
The state file stores all provider credentials securely
The state file is optional; Terraform can function without it by querying cloud providers directly each time

questions.explanation: The Terraform state file (terraform.tfstate) is the source of truth for what resources Terraform manages and their current attributes. It maps Terraform resource addresses (like 'aws_instance.web') to real-world resource IDs (like 'i-0abc1234'). Without state, Terraform couldn't determine which resources exist, what their current attributes are, or which resources need to be modified or deleted. The state also stores dependency metadata. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect — state is essential and should not store credentials.

questions.body: Why should you NOT commit the `terraform.tfstate` file to version control?

choices.text: Because the file is too large for git
Because the state file may contain sensitive data in plain text, and concurrent team members could overwrite each other's state, causing corruption
Because git cannot track JSON files
Because Terraform will automatically delete the state file if it detects a git repository

questions.explanation: There are two critical reasons not to commit terraform.tfstate to version control: 1) Security — the state file stores all resource attributes, including sensitive values like database passwords and private keys, in plain text. 2) Concurrency — if multiple team members pull and commit state files via git, they can overwrite each other's changes, leading to state corruption. The solution is to use a remote backend (like S3, Terraform Cloud, or Azure Blob Storage) with state locking. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect.

questions.body: What is state locking in Terraform and how does the Azure (`azurerm`) backend handle it?

choices.text: State locking encrypts the state file at rest; the azurerm backend provides this by default

State locking prevents concurrent state modifications by multiple users or processes. The azurerm backend uses Azure Blob Storage lease-based locking automatically; Terraform Cloud also provides locking automatically
State locking is a feature only available in Terraform Enterprise
State locking prevents anyone from reading the state file

questions.explanation: State locking prevents two Terraform operations from running simultaneously and corrupting the state. When an operation starts, Terraform acquires a lock on the state; other operations must wait. The azurerm backend uses Azure Blob Storage's native lease mechanism to implement locking automatically — no additional resource (like a DynamoDB table) is needed. Terraform Cloud and Terraform Enterprise also have built-in locking. The local backend implements locking using a .terraform.tfstate.lock.info file. Not all backends support locking — you should always choose one that does for team use. Options A, C, and D are incorrect.

questions.body: How do you configure Terraform to use an S3 bucket as a remote backend?

choices.text: Set the TF_BACKEND environment variable to the S3 bucket URL
Add a 'backend "s3"' block inside the 'terraform' block in your configuration, specifying the bucket, key, and region
Add a 'remote_state' block at the top level of your configuration
Run 'terraform backend s3 --bucket=my-bucket'

questions.explanation: Remote backends are configured within the 'terraform' block using a 'backend' block. For S3, you specify the bucket name, the key (path within the bucket for the state file), and region. Example: terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-tf-state" key = "prod/terraform.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" dynamodb_table = "tf-lock" } }. After changing the backend configuration, you must run 'terraform init' to migrate state. Options A, C, and D are all invalid syntax.

questions.body: What are Terraform workspaces used for?

choices.text: Workspaces allow you to use different cloud provider accounts simultaneously
Workspaces provide multiple named states within a single backend and configuration, typically used to manage multiple instances of the same infrastructure (e.g., dev, staging, prod)
Workspaces are separate GitHub repositories for Terraform code
Workspaces replace modules for code reuse

questions.explanation: Terraform workspaces allow you to have multiple state files for the same configuration, isolated by workspace name. Every Terraform configuration starts in the 'default' workspace. You can create new workspaces with 'terraform workspace new dev' and switch between them with 'terraform workspace select dev'. This enables managing multiple environments (dev/staging/prod) with the same codebase. In code, you can reference 'terraform.workspace' to vary behavior per workspace. Options A, C, D are all incorrect.

questions.body: What does `terraform state rm` do, and when would you use it?

choices.text: Destroys the specified resource and removes it from state
Removes a resource from Terraform state without destroying the real infrastructure, useful when you want to stop managing a resource without deleting it
Deletes the entire state file
Removes a resource from your configuration file

questions.explanation: 'terraform state rm <resource_address>' removes the specified resource from Terraform's state file without making any changes to the actual infrastructure. Use cases include: when you want to 'forget' about a resource (Terraform will no longer manage it), when migrating resources between Terraform configurations, or to work around a stuck resource during disaster recovery. After running state rm, if the resource block still exists in the configuration, running 'terraform plan' will show Terraform planning to create a new resource. Option A describes destroy. Option C is dangerous and not what this command does. Option D is editing configuration files.

questions.body: What is the `terraform_remote_state` data source used for?

choices.text: It migrates state from a local backend to a remote backend
It allows one Terraform configuration to read outputs from another configuration's remote state, enabling cross-stack references
It syncs state between multiple workspaces automatically
It backs up the state file to an S3 bucket

questions.explanation: The 'terraform_remote_state' data source lets you access outputs from another Terraform state file. For example, a networking configuration might output its VPC ID, and an application configuration can use 'data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.vpc_id' to reference it. This enables splitting large configurations into smaller, independently managed stacks while still being able to share values between them. This is a key pattern for large-scale infrastructure. Options A, C, D all describe things this data source does not do.

questions.body: A team member accidentally runs `terraform destroy` on the production environment. The state file shows all resources as destroyed. However, the actual cloud resources still exist (the destroy was blocked by IAM permissions). What should be done?

choices.text: Re-run terraform apply to recreate all resources from scratch
Use terraform import or terraform apply -refresh-only to reconcile state with actual infrastructure
The state is permanently corrupted; you must start over
Delete the state file and run terraform init

questions.explanation: When state doesn't match actual infrastructure (here the state says resources are gone but they actually exist), you need to reconcile state. Options include: 1) Restore a state backup (Terraform backends often keep versioned state). 2) Use 'terraform import' to re-import each resource. 3) Use 'terraform apply -refresh-only' to update state to reflect actual current infrastructure. Option A would try to create new resources (potentially duplicates). Option C is not true — state can be recovered. Option D loses all state information.

questions.body: What is the `moved` block in Terraform and when would you use it instead of `terraform state mv`?

choices.text: The moved block physically relocates a resource to a new cloud region

The moved block is declared in configuration and tells Terraform that a resource has been renamed or moved to a module, allowing state to be updated without manual CLI commands — making refactoring shareable through code
The moved block is only available in Terraform Enterprise
The moved block creates a copy of the resource at the new address and deletes the original

questions.explanation: The 'moved' block (introduced in Terraform 1.1) lets you document a resource rename or module restructure directly in your configuration code. When team members run 'terraform apply', Terraform automatically updates state to reflect the new address — no manual 'terraform state mv' command needed. This is superior for team workflows because the move instruction is version-controlled and applied consistently. Example: moved { from = azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.old_name  to = azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.new_name }. Option A is wrong — moved doesn't move cloud resources. Option C is wrong — it's available in open-source Terraform 1.1+. Option D is wrong — it's a state rename, not a copy.

questions.body: Which command would you use to show the current attributes of a specific Azure Linux virtual machine resource in Terraform state?

choices.text: terraform state show azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web
terraform show azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web
terraform inspect azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web
terraform state describe azurerm_linux_virtual_machine.web

questions.explanation: 'terraform state show <resource_address>' displays the current attributes of a specific resource as stored in the Terraform state file. This includes all known attributes like the resource ID, private IP addresses, tags, and other provider-specific attributes. This is useful for inspecting what Terraform knows about a resource without needing to open the state file directly. Option B ('terraform show') without 'state' shows the entire state or a saved plan file. Option C and D are not valid Terraform commands.

questions.body: What are Terraform built-in functions and where can they be used?

choices.text: Built-in functions can only be used in provider configuration blocks
Terraform has built-in functions (like join, split, tolist, length, file, etc.) that can be used in any expression within a Terraform configuration to transform and combine values
Terraform supports custom functions written in Go that users must compile and install
Built-in functions require the 'functions' provider to be installed

questions.explanation: Terraform has an extensive library of built-in functions for string manipulation (join, split, format, replace), collection manipulation (length, merge, flatten, tolist, toset), encoding (base64encode, jsonencode), filesystem (file, templatefile), math, date/time, and type conversion. These functions can be used in any expression — in resource arguments, locals, outputs, and variable defaults. They are evaluated at plan time. You can test them in 'terraform console'. Options A, C, D are all incorrect.

choices.text: A syntax error because you cannot use merge() on maps
A new map containing all key-value pairs from local.tags plus the additional key 'ManagedBy = terraform'
An overwrite of local.tags with only { ManagedBy = terraform }
A list containing both maps as separate items

questions.explanation: The 'merge()' function takes any number of maps and returns a single map containing all key-value pairs from all input maps. If multiple maps have the same key, later maps take precedence. In this example, 'merged_tags' will be a map with three keys: Environment, Project (from local.tags), and ManagedBy (from the inline map). This pattern is commonly used to add standard tags like 'ManagedBy' or 'CreatedAt' to all resources. Option A is wrong — merge works on maps. Option C is wrong — merge doesn't overwrite. Option D is wrong — the result is a single merged map.

questions.body: What is the purpose of the `templatefile()` function in Terraform?

choices.text: Renders a template file by substituting variable values, typically used for user_data scripts or configuration files
Creates a new Terraform configuration file from a template
Compresses a file and encodes it in base64
Downloads a template from the Terraform Registry

questions.explanation: The 'templatefile(path, vars)' function reads a template file and renders it with variable substitution using Terraform's template syntax. This is commonly used for EC2 user_data scripts, Kubernetes manifests, or configuration files that need to embed dynamic values. For example: user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/init.tpl", { hostname = var.hostname, region = var.region }). The template file uses ${variable_name} syntax for substitution. Options B, C, and D are all incorrect.

questions.body:
What does the following Terraform configuration do?

```hcl
resource "azurerm_network_security_group" "web" {
  name                = "web-nsg"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name

  dynamic "security_rule" {
    for_each = var.security_rules
    content {
      name                       = security_rule.value.name
      priority                   = security_rule.value.priority
      direction                  = security_rule.value.direction
      access                     = security_rule.value.access
      protocol                   = security_rule.value.protocol
      destination_port_range     = security_rule.value.destination_port_range
      source_address_prefix      = security_rule.value.source_address_prefix
      destination_address_prefix = security_rule.value.destination_address_prefix
    }
  }
}
```

choices.text: Creates multiple network security group resources, one per security rule
Dynamically generates multiple nested 'security_rule' blocks within the azurerm_network_security_group resource based on the values in var.security_rules
Creates a for loop that iterates but does nothing with the values
This syntax is invalid; Terraform does not support dynamic blocks

questions.explanation: Dynamic blocks allow you to generate repeated nested blocks (like 'security_rule' blocks in an azurerm_network_security_group) based on a collection variable. The 'for_each' expression iterates over 'var.security_rules', and for each element, generates one 'security_rule' block with the specified content. Inside the content block, 'security_rule.value' refers to the current iteration's value (or 'security_rule.key' for the key). This avoids hardcoding a fixed number of nested blocks. Option A is wrong — it's creating blocks within ONE resource. Options C and D are incorrect.

questions.body: What is the `for` expression in Terraform and provide an example of what `[for s in var.names : upper(s)]` produces?

choices.text: A for expression is a loop that modifies resources in place. [for s in var.names : upper(s)] renames all resources
A for expression creates a new list or map by transforming a collection. [for s in var.names : upper(s)] produces a new list with all names converted to uppercase
For expressions can only be used in variable declarations
[for s in var.names : upper(s)] is a syntax error

questions.explanation: The 'for' expression in Terraform creates a new list or map by transforming each element of an input collection. '[for s in var.names : upper(s)]' takes each element 's' from var.names and applies the upper() function, producing a new list with all strings in uppercase. For example, if var.names = ["alice", "bob"], the result is ["ALICE", "BOB"]. For maps, the syntax is '{for k, v in var.map : k => upper(v)}'. Options A, C, and D are all incorrect.

questions.body:
What does the `sensitive` argument on a variable declaration do?

```hcl
variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}
```

choices.text: Encrypts the variable value in the Terraform state file
Marks the value as sensitive so Terraform redacts it in plan and apply output, preventing it from appearing in logs
Prevents the variable from being passed to child modules
Requires the variable value to be a minimum of 8 characters

questions.explanation: Declaring a variable as 'sensitive = true' tells Terraform to redact its value in all CLI output, plan output, and state output. This prevents sensitive values from appearing in terminal logs or CI/CD pipeline output. However, the value is still passed to resources and modules normally, and it is still stored in the state file in plain text — so the state file must be protected. If a sensitive variable is used in a resource argument, that attribute in the plan will also be shown as '(sensitive value)'. Options A, C, D are all incorrect.

questions.body: What are the different ways to provide values for Terraform input variables? List them in order of precedence (highest to lowest).

choices.text: Only via -var CLI flag; no other methods are supported
Environment variables (TF_VAR_) → .tfvars files → -var-file flag → -var flag → default value
-var flag → -var-file flag → *.auto.tfvars and terraform.tfvars → TF_VAR_ environment variables → default value (highest to lowest)
default value → TF_VAR_ environment variables → -var flag (highest to lowest)

questions.explanation: Terraform variable values can be set through multiple mechanisms, with the following precedence (highest wins): 1) -var CLI flag (highest). 2) -var-file CLI flag. 3) *.auto.tfvars and terraform.tfvars files in the configuration directory (auto-loaded). 4) TF_VAR_<name> environment variables. 5) Variable default values in the declaration (lowest). When multiple mechanisms are used, higher precedence values override lower ones. This order lets you set defaults in .tfvars files but override them on the command line for specific runs.

questions.body: What is the `file()` function in Terraform used for, and how is it different from `templatefile()`?

choices.text: file() and templatefile() are identical
file() reads a file and returns its contents as a string without any variable substitution. templatefile() reads a file and renders it as a template with variable substitution
file() can only read binary files; templatefile() can only read text files
file() is deprecated; always use templatefile()

questions.explanation: The 'file(path)' function reads the contents of a file and returns it as a string, with no processing or substitution. This is useful for reading static content like TLS certificates, SSH public keys, or scripts that don't need dynamic values. The 'templatefile(path, vars)' function also reads a file but processes it as a template, replacing '${var_name}' placeholders with values from the 'vars' map. Use 'file()' for static content and 'templatefile()' when you need to inject dynamic values. Options A, C, D are all incorrect.
